_note: Authentication / authorization checkpoints. Cross-domain — any app with user-scoped resources reuses these. Checkpoints define WHAT to check; an archetype manifest assigns severity (HOW MUCH it matters there).

checkpoints.title: Ownership checks on user-scoped resources
checkpoints.why: IDOR - accessing another user's record (order, profile, document) by changing an id - is invisible externally and a top AI-code hole.

levels.3: Resources fetched by id with no ownership check.
levels.6: Every user-scoped read/write verifies the resource belongs to the requester.
levels.9: Centralized authorization enforced by default, with tests proving cross-account access is denied.

verify.reasoning: For endpoints fetching a resource by id (e.g. order, profile, document, payment): does each verify ownership? Can user A reach user B's record by changing the id?

assertions.check: User-scoped fetches filter by owner id, not resource id alone.
A test attempts cross-account access and gets 403/404.

checkpoints.title: Sessions and auth tokens are managed safely
checkpoints.why: AI-generated auth often ships sessions that never expire, logouts that don't invalidate server-side, or password-reset tokens that are guessable or long-lived - each a quiet account-takeover path.

levels.3: Long-lived or non-expiring sessions; logout only clears the client; reset tokens predictable or without expiry.
levels.6: Sessions expire and can be invalidated server-side on logout; tokens are random and short-lived; auth cookies are HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite.
levels.9: Rotation on privilege change, idle + absolute timeouts, server-side session revocation, single-use expiring reset tokens, with tests covering logout/expiry.

verify.reasoning: How long do sessions live and can they be revoked server-side? Does logout invalidate the session on the server or only the client? Are password-reset/verification tokens random, single-use, and short-lived? Are auth cookies HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite?

assertions.check: Session/token has a server-side expiry and a revocation path (logout invalidates server-side).
Auth cookies set HttpOnly and Secure (and SameSite).
Password-reset/verification tokens are cryptographically random, single-use, and expiring.

checkpoints.title: Passwords stored with a strong, salted, slow hash
checkpoints.why: AI-generated auth routinely stores passwords in plaintext, hashes them with a fast unsalted digest (md5/sha1/bare sha256), or even encrypts them reversibly - so one database leak becomes a full credential breach and fuels credential-stuffing everywhere the user reused that password.

levels.3: Passwords stored in plaintext, reversibly encrypted, or hashed with a fast/unsalted digest (md5, sha1, bare sha256).
levels.6: Passwords hashed with a slow, salted, adaptive algorithm (bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2) with a per-user salt; login verification is constant-time.
levels.9: Argon2id/bcrypt with tuned cost, a pepper or KMS-backed secret where warranted, rehash-on-login as parameters harden, and no password ever logged.

verify.reasoning: How are passwords stored? Plaintext, a reversible cipher, or a fast digest (md5/sha1/sha256)? Or a slow adaptive hash (bcrypt/scrypt/argon2) with a per-user salt? Is the login comparison constant-time, and is any password ever logged?

assertions.check: Passwords are hashed with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 - not plaintext, reversible encryption, or a fast/unsalted digest.
Each password uses a unique salt and login verification is constant-time.
